title: Red tape stifling AI boom
full_text:
A data centre entrepreneur has warned that red tape and decades of failed policy are preventing Australia from becoming a global leader in the artificial intelligence boom.
Dan Roberts, a former Macquarie banker who co-founded data centre firm Iren with his brother Will in 2018, has bemoaned the state of Australia’s investment environment.
In its previous life as a bitcoin miner, the former Iris Energy was turned away by the ASX when the company looked to list in 2021.
That proved fortuitous. Instead, Iren floated on the tech-heavy Nasdaq in the US, where Mr Roberts said that on Friday it “traded $US10bn in a single session … more than the entire ASX’s on-market daily turnover of $7.5bn”.
Their rollercoaster ride back from a $US1 share price was documented in The List – Australia’s Richest 250 in March, when they debuted with an estimated $972m joint fortune.
“I’ll be honest. That number is humbling. But it also stings a little. Because two-and-a-half years ago, we were told we weren’t welcome on the ASX,” Mr Roberts wrote on LinkedIn.
“The rejection was disappointing – not just for what it meant for us as a company, but for what it said about Australia’s willingness to back next-generation technology at scale. We believed then, as we do now, that the future of digital infrastructure and compute deserved a place in Australian capital markets. Apparently, the feeling wasn’t mutual.” The surge in Iren’s share price came after it was announced the Sydney-headquartered firm had signed a $4.7bn deal with Nvidia, the world’s most valuable company, to house its processing chips in Iren data centres.
Mr Roberts insisted he and Will had “never given up on Australia” and Iren had plans to build several large data centres across the country.
“Australia has everything it needs to be a global leader in AI infrastructure – the land, the renewable energy, the engineering talent,” he wrote. “(But) the permitting and regulatory process remains our biggest challenge – and I won’t pretend otherwise. It is slow, complex and, at times, deeply frustrating for a business operating at the speed that AI demands.
“What it needs now is the regulatory and policy environment to not miss out on this opportunity.” It is not the first time Mr Roberts has blamed the regulatory environment for stifling innovation. In October last year, he said capital markets in Australia were not doing enough to support tech growth.
“We’ve got to put a framework in place to catalyse this opportunity,” Mr Roberts said. Iren was memorably prevented from listing on the ASX because of the local bourse’s position on the valuation of bitcoin on its balance sheet. But Mr Roberts previously said he clearly told the ASX at the time that the company does not hold bitcoin – although it was exposed to the cryptocurrency through mining.
summary: Data centre entrepreneur Dan Roberts highlights that excessive regulation is hindering Australia's potential to lead in the artificial intelligence sector.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Roberts criticises Australia's investment environment for stifling technological innovation.
Iren, co-founded by Roberts, successfully listed on the Nasdaq after being rejected by the ASX.
The company has signed a significant deal with Nvidia, but faces challenges from slow regulatory processes.
relevance: Understanding the regulatory landscape is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals involved in technology investments and innovation.
